speaker: interviewer
text: This is August 3rd, 1995. I'm Mary Rothschild interviewing Mr. Lipman Radzik for the Survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is August 3rd, 1995, tape number one with Mr. Lipman Radzik. Please give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Lipman Radzik, spell L-I-P-M-A-N. And my family name is Radzik, spelled R-A-D-Z-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was this always your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Always.

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born June 5th, 1921.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, city of the name Zuromin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Spelled [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Z-U-R-O-M-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it close to any large city in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was very close, actually, to the city of Mlawa. And it was very close also to Warsaw, the capital city of Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember from your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was brought up in a very strictly religious home, very closely knit, well-loved, well-cared. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a very loving, beautiful family.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three brothers and four sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Their name-- the oldest one was Esther. The second name was Henech. The third one was Sima. The fourth was [? Hersch. ?] Herschek we used to call him. And the next one was Rivka. And the next one was [? Hava. ?] And the young one was Beresh. That was the seven children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you grow up in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: We grow up in a house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was a very beautiful home. And I remember the-- the creek and the-- even the-- the few trees that we have, every stone, was-- which was very-- was very-- very beautiful surrounding. You have to realize that people who had lived in Europe, there was not a moving. Your parents, your grandparents-- for generations we lived in the same place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it become, as we call it, the mother country, the Mother Earth. It was a part of you, of your birth. So every part was known to you. What was your father's occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a very learned man. He spoke a number of languages. And besides this, he was-- he-- [INAUDIBLE] he was selling merchandise of-- how do you buy? You buy clothes by the yard, you know, that people will buy. And that kind of thing was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I remember [INAUDIBLE] my father-- there are so many things I can say about my father, blessed memory. He had a tremendous love and tremendous caring for many people. And he was very highly respected in our city. People used to come to him for advice. And I remember as a little boy, I used to get myself into many mischievous things as a young boy. As my father used to say to me-- and I have to relate that particular episode-- of course, I was lying to defend myself. It wasn't my fault. It was always others. It was always Mary's fault or other ones. It was always-- so my father said to me, by not admitting it, your guilt, you most probably will do it again. And by admitting it, what you have done, you will put by yourself to guard yourself not to do it again. And by saying it-- I'm sorry, I did something I shouldn't have done-- it will protect you. And it'll-- and I was preaching the same thing to my children. It was something that just-- I have many episodes from my dear father, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's name was Malka. My mother also was a very intelligent man and a very beautiful woman, as you can see to the picture. And when she walked with my sisters, it was hardly you could tell who was the mother and who was the sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister used to get jealous. She said, Mother, you're not getting any older. We are getting older. She was a very wonderful human being. They would-- so many people used to come to her. And she used to do many charitable things unknowingly that nobody will know about it. That she would-- it was a part of life to do it. You didn't live for yourself. There is somebody else in this world than you. [INAUDIBLE] A very beautiful human being, my mother. I-- [CRYING] find it [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] very difficult to accept [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that she's no more [? among the living. ?] How much do you remember about the Shabbat in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Shabbat was something-- a most beautiful-- every Shabbat was something new. It wasn't the same Shabbat it was before. You-- the whole week of Shabbat, you start preparing it from Sunday on. That next Shabbat, my blessed mother-- I'm going to make this and make this. It was something of a sort of pleasing. She usually used to know. All of her children would know a certain kind of cooking of a certain thing that she would make, always something. And her greatest enjoyment was it came out good. Or the cholent come out good or the challahs, because in Europe, as you know, there wasn't such a thing that you buy anything ready-made. It was always you made everything from scratch. And each one has their own way of ingredients, of doing things, or making. And my mother was the greatest cook and the greatest-- of course, we had a maid in home, which did-- but my mother always looked different Shabbat. She was like the queen of Shabbat. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: She had such a radiant look on her face, such a glow or something. It's very difficult to-- to explain it. It was something beautiful. And well, of course, in the Shabbat, how could you sit down by yourself? You always had to have some guests too. And my mother was always to make sure that-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that there's somebody there, and they liked the food and things like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat was something very beautiful. And of course, the-- the Shabbat meal was-- it wasn't only the food. It was a table where Torah and world affairs and love and laughter and singing together. It was not that you ate, and you got up from the table. It was something we never know it-- the table, the Shabbat table, lingered on unknown hours. And everybody had to sit and share or contributed to part of the Shabbat table, something like this. This is indescribable. It's very beautiful.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we speak, of course-- we speak most often Yiddish by the table. And of course, we speak some other languages, because my-- they speak the language, Polish language. Or my mother speak very good German. A part of her family come from Germany. It was a mixture of exchanging it [INAUDIBLE]. And it was a lot of laughter and-- and-- and happiness. There was-- it was all interwoven one with each other. It was-- was a part of us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [DRINKS] Where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first, our city was not a big city. Of course, I went to school at the high school in my city. And then of course, I was sent to another school. I went to Lodz-- was the biggest city. We had some relatives there. When I went to the bigger, larger Yeshiva there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say maybe 16, 17, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you like being in the Yeshiva? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I liked it very much. Of course, we become a very close knit family. And we're always afraid-- in other words, to make it-- put it bluntly-- that my mother would never let you grow up. You're always that little boy. And I used to have that kind of fight.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I'm now 12 years old. I'm 13 years now, 14 years old. And mother was always, like-- like, hovering over you, you know, like covering you with her lovable wings over you, you know? You shouldn't-- it's always to worry for anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, we used to come home for the holidays. And we used to speak on the telephone and letters and things. It's always close to home and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your favorite subjects at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at school there was different subjects according, you know, to age. When you got older, you got different subject and different things and-- and the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the horizon of knowledge opened much more brighter. You would want to know this.

speaker: interviewer
text: As the old saying goes, that how much is a person supposed to know to realize how little he knows? You know, you [INAUDIBLE] and say, my God. I don't [INAUDIBLE] like to have this. You would like to-- like a person which is thirsty, more how much you want to know, more and more. I was-- I loved to read a lot. What did you read?

speaker: interviewee
text: I read many different Talmudical things and secular things. And my mother and father always encouraged us to know things much more, that we should learn more. The book was a part of your daily life. And how many time my mother, blessed memory, she used to come at night, [LAUGHS] in the middle of the night. And she'd find me reading at a certain book. She said, it's 2 o'clock in the morning. [INAUDIBLE], you have to go tomorrow to school. And sometime I will quickly cover the book, and I put it under my pillow and pretend that I slept. But I could never fool my mother. She knew it, that I was reading.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, certain things were just-- I-- you have to realize that to merit this kind of interview, it's-- we call it, this is your life. And there is so much for-- and I have a problem with me. What shall I tell you first? How long did you stay at the Yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I stayed the Yeshiva till I was-- till actually, to my knowledge, till actually nearly till 1939, till the war, the year before things were starting getting very unstable. And my mother insisted that I should come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had only my grandfather from my mother's side. Unfortunately, my father's parents have died. I don't recall them. Must have died young. My grandfather was a special. He had such a patriarch look. You know, it's a white beard. It's blue eyes. And he had such a love and such a caring. He was-- to bring you just a little episode from my grandfather, I remember that my sister was-- you know, [INAUDIBLE] was [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, they were somebody. She was going with a certain boy of course. Well, let's find out whom she is going out. So my sister was very worried. And they noticed that most probably this will lead to a-- they should get engaged. And I remember it was a young boy. She was-- had one worry-- that he should find favor in my grandfather's eyes. She wasn't so much worried for my parents, but she-- because my grandfather was the patriarch of the family. What he said was holy. That was her worry. I only hope that my grandfather would like him. He was a wonderful, wonderful human being. Unfortunately, he was murdered in-- in the ghetto. He had the white beard. And the German, they-- they caught him. And they shot him. They killed him in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What incidents of anti-Semitism do you remember before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, to be truthfully speaking that I remember, I encounter a number of anti-Semitism in Poland. Maybe you were going from school. Of course, and we were going to secular schools, which were not only Jewish, but Gentile. And they used to call you all kind of different names and different things because the Jewish student accelerate much more better in different subject. And there was the jealousy a lot. And sometimes they would beat us up. But I remember one time I come back, and my shirt was-- was torn because I had a fight with somebody there. And my mother was terrible. And so I said to her, you see-- see my shirt is torn. You should have seen what I did to him. So it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: --many thing we had to encounter, many thing that was anti-Semitism [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] against the Jews. And they felt like most Poland was on border with the Germans. And there were many anti-Semites in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that time when your shirt was torn?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had a fight, a physical fight--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: --with him because I didn't want to run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- did they say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: They said something mean. He was antagonizing me a number of time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said certain words, you know. It wasn't only that he antagonized me or he said anti-Semitic cursing at myself, but also at my people. And I felt like I had to stand up for the pride and to defend my people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he say?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said many-- I don't remember exact words. But he said that the Jewish people are [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sucking the blood of the Polish-- all that kind of thing. I don't remember exactly the right word, [INAUDIBLE] but it was very anti-Semitic slurs and things like that. So many of them run away. But I said, I'm not going to run away from it. And I'll be truthfully speaking. I paid him back in a physical way. He never started to me again.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been about-- I would say about 12, 13, something like this. Now, the interesting part was that I developed after more friends from my school that I was the one, the hero, that I didn't let them get away with it, with that kind of thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a Jewish neighborhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the city was a mixed neighborhood from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were the relationships between the Jews and the non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was many anti-Semites. And of course, there were many nice people. We had very wonderful-- my father had many kind Polish people, which he dealt with them. And they were very nice and very-- but this anti-Semitism is very deep.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you cannot sometime tell what lies in the back of your mind. But a part of it was told-- not to bring this in to you-- that why did Hitler brought Jews from Holland to be exterminated in Poland? Why did he bring Jews from France, from all the Scandinavian countries to Poland from others, from there? Why Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they knew that Poland were the place the anti-Semitism was so-- not only they didn't-- what do you call it-- object, they actually were, which helped to the extermination of the six million Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the beginning of the war, I have to make that following statement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the Germans occupied our town, we were right on the border of Germany. And they came in actually the second day to our city. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And all the horrors would start to come in. And people from-- witnesses from others to tell what's going on. People didn't want to believe. They didn't believe that something like this is going to happen. The misleading part was-- I have to go back a little bit to history. The First World War--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Radzik, where were you on September 1st, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in the city of Zuromin, Zuromin.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you experience? What did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: I experienced-- because people-- there was such a panic that people start to run away to other city, which were not occupied by that time. And of course, after the Germans caught up with us-- and the first encounter I remember was that I met a column of German Army soldiers. And they were having a wagon of horses. They were the occupation. And the first thing they start screaming is that we should push the wagons of those, which were-- had horses on it. And they were hysterically laughing that we can-- don't need the horses. The Jews will carry the wagons. But I just want to go back. But I said before--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you-- did you-- were they speaking in German?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were speaking in German. And I understood--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: --quite well. The humiliation was terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you forced to do this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was forced to do that. And he was holding a whip. He went down, which, from that wagon, and he was hitting us with a whip.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think? What did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terribly humiliating. It was terrible thing. We couldn't believe one human being would do this to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were then 18 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the next memory you have after the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the next memory was that we didn't stay long in Zuromin there. They forced us out in one night. The night before, I remember, was Simchas Torah. And [? we had the shul. ?] They burned the synagogue. They have a very beautiful synagogue. And synagogue was sitting in the middle of the marketplace. Synagogue was not attached to another building. It was a building by itself. And I remember like today, [INAUDIBLE] the flames were coming out from the windows from where they burned. And the following day, they run from, with the help from Polish citizens, and they knocked on the doors. And we had to leave everything. They didn't even give you any-- whatever you had on yourself. I remember I didn't have even an overcoat because that was in-- in September. That's right. There was still no [INAUDIBLE] after the High Holidays. And they forced us to march by foot to the Warsaw ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many members of your family were with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the family was my parents. There was my brother. There was my brother and my two sisters. The other family lived in Mlawa where my grandfather lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the Warsaw ghetto from where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's-- by train, it was actually about an hour and a half. And we were-- half of it we were-- we were forced to go to-- to-- by foot. And it was a terrible-- the screams of those children and women. It was the most horrifying scene-- indescribable to tell what this kind of march was. That was the beginning from the horror of the beginning of the horrifying things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take you till you got to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it took maybe about two days, or something like this. It was horrifying, I think. And of course, along the way, whatever you had something you grabbed, the Germans and the-- and then the Polish Nazis, whenever they attacked you, they took all your possessions away, if you had any money or jewelry, whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened after you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we arrived in the Warsaw, you have to realize there was thousands of refugees from other different cities. I know there was a tremendous-- there was nowhere, where every synagogue and every place, every hole was occupied by people. Yesterday you were living in-- two days ago-- in your home. You had everything. The next day, you had no shirt to change. You had nothing. You were displaced. You were just-- absolutely nothing, you had. It was a terrible encounter just to preserve yourself. It was the most horrifying thing. I remember, we lived in a-- in a synagogue with many other people. There was no place where to sleep. And each one was just lying on the floor. It was horrible-- no food, nothing. It was a horrible thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were there-- I was there [DRINKS] maybe about a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during this year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, during that year, you were forced to go to-- to work. The Germans took you out to work of many other places to work for them. And it was-- the Warsaw ghetto was very crowded. It was the most horrifying of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find another place to live in? Or did you stay in the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as a matter of fact, we find some of his distant relatives. My mother had a brother in Warsaw. And so we then tried to live the little time with them. And from the bombing before, all their windows were broken. They had no heating. They had no water. Everything was there. And it was the most horrifying [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] experience. By the way, of that brother, I want to mention that I still have a cousin now in Poland. She lives in a city of [? Cieszyn. ?] And she is the only one survivor, actually. She was the daughter of the uncle. He was a brother to my mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the Warsaw ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a fight of to survival to get something, to stand in line to be able to get a piece of bread or whatever. The hunger was terrible. And the-- no medical care, no medical-- it was-- it was the most horrifying things. I would like to bring to you a certain episode when I was in a German [? commune. ?] Because Warsaw was a very big city, and it had a big yard. When-- and there was houses. I remember the [INAUDIBLE] in the big cities. And the-- the two Germans came in there. And they would start screaming, Juden, raus, that people should come out there, when they were looking for work. And they start shooting at-- with their guns in their hands. And it was the most horrifying things. I have to relate this to-- to those future generations. They should know what mine eyes actually have seen. And I find it even now difficult to be able to tell what mine eyes have seen. I couldn't accept it mentally. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we continue, we might as well change--
